title: Two Networks, Opposite Goals
concept: The generator and discriminator train together but pursue opposite goals: the generator wants to fool the discriminator, while the discriminator wants to catch every fake — a competitive game, not a cooperative partnership.

exercises.prompt: Recap: which network's job is to decide whether an image is real or fake?

options.text: Neither — that decision is made by hand

exercises.explain: Right — judging real vs. fake is the discriminator's whole purpose.

cards.title: Not on the same team
cards.body: So far you've met two networks separately. Here's the twist: they aren't collaborators. They're trained together, but they want completely opposite things — like two players in the same game with opposite win conditions.

cards.title: A competitive game, not teamwork
cards.body: GAN training is a competitive game: the generator's goal is to fool the discriminator into believing its fakes are real. The discriminator's goal is to catch every fake and correctly flag every real image. One network's win is the other network's loss.

cards.title: Striker vs. goalkeeper
cards.body: Think of a soccer match: the striker's only job is to get the ball past the goalkeeper and into the net. The goalkeeper's only job is to block every single shot. They're on the same field, playing the same game, but their goals are exact opposites — one player's goal is the other's nightmare. That's the generator (the striker, trying to sneak fakes past) and the discriminator (the goalkeeper, trying to block every one).

cards.title: One network's win is the other's loss
cards.body: If the discriminator correctly spots a fake image, that's a win for the discriminator and a failure for the generator. If the generator's fake slips through and the discriminator is fooled, that's a win for the generator and a failure for the discriminator.

cards.title: They aren't working toward a shared goal
cards.body: It's tempting to think both networks are "on the same team," cooperating to make good images. They're not. If they cooperated, the generator could just ask the discriminator for tips — but the discriminator's entire purpose is to resist being fooled by it.

left.text:
To fool the discriminator
Make fakes pass as real

right.text:
To catch every fake
Never be tricked

exercises.prompt: What is the generator's goal in this game?

options.text: To fool the discriminator into thinking its fakes are real
To help the discriminator classify images correctly
To copy real training images exactly
To minimize its own image quality

exercises.explain: Right — fooling the discriminator is the generator's entire objective.

exercises.prompt: True or false: the generator and discriminator are cooperating toward the same objective.
exercises.explain: False — they have opposite goals. One network's success is the other's failure.

exercises.sentence: GAN training is best described as a ___ game between two networks with opposite goals.
exercises.explain: Competitive — not cooperative. That's the core idea behind the word "adversarial."

exercises.prompt: A discriminator correctly flags a generator's newest image as fake. Whose "win" is this round?

options.text: Neither network "wins" or "loses" in GAN training

exercises.explain: Catching a fake is exactly what the discriminator is trying to do — that's a win for it, and a setback for the generator.

exercises.prompt: In a single training step, your discriminator confidently labels a brand-new image from the generator as "fake" (a score near 0). From the competitive-game framing you just learned, what does this round mean, and what happens next for the generator?

options.text: The discriminator won this round; the generator will use that failure as a signal to adjust its weights so its next attempt is more convincing
The generator won this round, since it produced an image at all
Both networks stop training, since a fake was detected
The discriminator's weights are reset back to random

exercises.explain: Exactly! In this adversarial game, getting caught is a loss for the generator, and it responds by improving. Next: see exactly how those adjustments happen, one alternating turn at a time.
